learning_objectives: Describir the pathophysiology of the basal ganglia-tálamo-cortical circuit in Parkinson's disease and the mechanism of DBS therapeutic effect
Aplicar the UPDRS and LSVT scales to assess PD severity and DBS candidacy criteria
Comparar the major DBS targets for PD (STN, GPi) in terms of motor outcomes, medication reduction, and cognitive/neuropsychiatric side effects
Identificar the DBS targets and outcomes for essential tremor (VIM), dystonia (GPi), and OCD/depression (VC/VS, ALIC, SCC)
Describir the surgical technique, lead placement verification, and common post-operative complications of DBS implantation
Explicar the emerging indications for DBS including Tourette syndrome, Alzheimer's disease (fornix), and obesity (LH)

sections.title: Circuito de Ganglios Basales y Mecanismo de la ECP

blocks.content:
Deep brain stimulation (DBS) delivers continuous or intermittent high-frequency electrical stimulation (typically 130-185 Hz, 60-90 μs pulse width, 1-5 V) through chronically implanted electrodes to specific subcortical targets. The therapeutic mechanism was initially hypothesised to be 'functional ablation' mimicking lesioning, but is now understood to involve complex modulation of neural circuits: (1) **inhibition of local neuronal activity** (hyperpolarising the stimulated nucleus); (2) **excitation of efferent axons** projecting from the target; (3) **disruption of abnormal oscillatory activity** (particularly pathological beta-band synchrony in PD, 13-30 Hz); (4) **modulation of network-level activity** across the entire basal ganglia-tálamo-cortical loop. These effects are reversible — cessation of stimulation typically restores the pre-stimulation state within hours, providing a key safety advantage over ablative lesioning.

The **basal ganglia-tálamo-cortical circuit** comprises parallel loops relevant to movement, cognition, and limbic function. The **direct pathway**: striatum (D1 receptors, GABA) → globus pallidus interna (GPi) / substantia nigra pars reticulata (SNr) — INHIBITORY; GPi/SNr → thalamus (VL) — INHIBITORY; net effect of direct pathway: disinhibition of thalamus → facilitation of cortical movement. The **indirect pathway**: striatum (D2 receptors, GABA) → globus pallidus externa (GPe) — INHIBITORY; GPe → subtalámico nucleus (STN) — INHIBITORY; STN → GPi/SNr — EXCITATORY; GPi/SNr → thalamus — INHIBITORY; net effect of indirect pathway: inhibition of thalamus → suppression of cortical movement. In **Parkinson's disease**, dopamine loss disproportionately reduces D1 striatal activity (less direct pathway facilitation) and reduces D2-mediated inhibition of the indirect pathway (more STN activity) — resulting in excessive GPi/SNr inhibition of the thalamus, causing bradykinesia and rigidity. DBS of the STN reduces thes excesivo STN excitatory output to GPi, and DBS of GPi directly reduces GPi inhibition of the thalamus, both restoring balanced tálamo-cortical facilitation.

blocks.title: Dianas de ECP e Indicaciones Primarias
rows: Reduces STN overactivity → reduces GPi inhibition → restores talámico drive; allows medication reduction
Directly reduces GPi output → disinhibits thalamus; less medication reduction in PD vs STN
Suppresses talámico relay of tremor oscillations; does NOT improve bradykinesia/rigidity
Anterior limb of internal capsule + ventral striatum
Suppresses hyperactive default mode network; reduces depression severity
Modulates cortico-thalamo-striatal circuits controlling vocalisations and motor tics

blocks.title: Mecanismo de ECP — No es Simplemente Ablación Funcional
blocks.content: DBS at high frequency (130+ Hz) is NOT simply equivalent to a lesion. Key differences: (1) reversible — turning off stimulation restores baseline; (2) frequency-dependent effects — low frequency (<50 Hz) can WORSEN symptoms (facilitating instead of inhibiting pathological circuits); (3) adaptive stimulation (closed-loop DBS) can vary parameters in response to beta-band LFP activity; (4) axonal excitation at the target can drive downstream circuits in complex ways. The reversibility of DBS is its primary safety advantage over ablative procedures (pallidotomy, thalamotomy).

sections.title: ECP en la Enfermedad de Parkinson: NST vs GPi

blocks.content:
DBS is the gold-standard surgical treatment for **motor complications** of Parkinson's disease — specifically drug-induced dyskinesia and motor fluctuations (off periods) in patients with good levodopa response who are no longer adequately controlled on optimised medication. The landmark evidence comes from two major trials.

**VA/NINDS Randomised Ensayo (Weaver et al. JAMA 2009)**: 255 PD patients randomised to DBS (STN or GPi, surgeon's choice) vs best medical management. At 6 months: **DBS group gained 4.6 hours/day more 'good control' time (ON without troublesome dyskinesia)** vs 0 hours in medical group (P<0.001). Motor scores (UPDRS III off medication) improved 27% with DBS vs no improvement with medical management.

**EARLYSTIM Ensayo (Schuepbach et al. NEJM 2013)**: 251 younger PD patients (age <60, early motor complications, mean disease duration 7.3 years) randomised to STN-DBS vs best medical therapy. Primary outcome: PDQ-39 quality of life score. **STN-DBS superior at 24 months: PDQ-39 improved 7.8 points vs worsened 0.2 points in medical group (P<0.001)**. This trial extended DBS candidacy to younger patients with early (but significant) motor complications, challenging the 'save DBS for late-stage disease' paradigm.

**STN vs GPi comparison** (COMPARE trial and VA trial subanalyses): Both targets achieve equivalent motor outcomes in PD. Key differences: **STN-DBS** allows greater reduction in dopaminergic medications (mean 50-60% reduction in levodopa equivalent dose), which reduces dyskinesia and medication costs; **GPi-DBS** provides better direct dyskinesia suppression, better mood outcomes (less depression), and is preferred in patients with pre-existing cognitive impairment or depression, as STN stimulation se asocia con neuropsychiatric side effects (anxiety, hypomania, depression). **Seguridad cognitiva**: both STN and GPi DBS are associated with small declines in verbal fluency (~10% decline), but STN carries greater risk of neuropsychiatric complications. For elderly patients (>70) or those with pre-existing cognitive impairment, **GPi is preferred**.

**DBS candidacy criteria for PD**: (1) clear PD diagnosis (not atypical parkinsonism — MSA, PSP, DLB); (2) significant motor complications (>2 hours/day off time, or disabling dyskinesia) despite optimised medical management; (3) good levodopa response (>30% improvement in UPDRS motor score with levodopa challenge — predicts response to STN-DBS); (4) no significant cognitive impairment (MoCA ≥24, neuropsychological assessment); (5) no untreated psychiatric illness (depression, psychosis, suicidality); (6) no significant structural brain pathology; (7) patient understanding of device management requirements.

blocks.title: ECP-NST vs ECP-GPi en la Enfermedad de Parkinson
rows: Similar verbal fluency decline; generally safer in mild CI
Younger, cognitively intact, high medication burden
Elderly, mild cognitive impairment, depression, dyskinesia dominant
Longer (lower energy requirements in some patients)

blocks.title: Contraindicaciones Absolutas de la ECP en EP
blocks.content: DBS should NOT be performed in: (1) atypical parkinsonism (MSA — multi-system atrophy; PSP — progressive supranuclear palsy; CBD — corticobasal degeneration; DLB — dementia with Lewy bodies) — these do not respond to levodopa and will not respond to STN/GPi DBS; (2) significant dementia (MoCA <24, formal neuropsychological impairment); (3) untreated depression or psychosis; (4) poor levodopa response (<30% UPDRS improvement) — this is the single most reliable predictor of DBS failure. The Levodopa Challenge Test is mandatory: pre-op UPDRS motor score in off state vs post-levodopa on state — the response to DBS mirrors the response to levodopa.

sections.title: DBS Surgical Technique and Lead Placement

blocks.content:
DBS implantation is typically performed in two stages. **Stage 1**: bilateral (or unilateral) lead implantation via estereotáctico con marco (Leksell, Cosman-Roberts-Wells frames) or sin marco (Nexframe, robot-assisted) approaches under local anaesthesia with light sedation (awake surgery) or general anaesthesia. Awake surgery enables **microelectrode recording (MER)** and **macrostimulation testing**: MER identifies the target nucleus by its characteristic neuronal firing pattern (STN: high-frequency 'irregular' discharges at 20-50 Hz, typical 'cicada' sound; GPi: high-frequency ~90 Hz; talámico VIM: burst firing); macrostimulation through the electrode at progressively higher voltages tests for therapeutic effect and side effects (internal capsule stimulation: capsular contraction — 'spread to pyramidal tract'; sensory thalamus: paraesthesiae). **Stage 2** (typically 1-3 weeks later): subcutaneous implantable pulse generator (IPG) placement, usually infraclavicular, with subcutaneous extension cable tunnelled to the lead connectors in the scalp.

For **MRI-guided focused ultrasound (MRgFUS) thalamotomy**, an alternative to VIM DBS for essential tremor, no implant is placed — focused ultrasound energy is delivered through the intact skull (1024-element phased array transducer) using the ExAblate Neuro system, creating a small thermal lesion (55-60°C for 10-20 seconds) at the VIM. MRI thermometry provides real-time temperature monitoring. This is a single-session outpatient procedure with immediate tremor control in ~70-80% of patients, but the effect is UNILATERAL (bilateral MRgFUS risks ataxia and speech disturbances) and PERMANENT (unlike DBS). FDA approved in 2016 for essential tremor.

Post-operative MRI or CT confirms lead placement. **Programming** begins 2-4 weeks post-implantation after tissue oedema resolution. Programming parameters: contact selection (which of the 4 DBS contacts to activate), polarity (monopolar vs bipolar), amplitude (V), frequency (Hz), pulse width (μs). Programming is iterative — initial settings are conservative with progressive adjustment over 3-12 months. Key complications: **haemorrhage** (1-3% symptomatic intracranial haemorrhage from lead placement), **infection** (3-5% hardware infection, often requiring device explantation), **lead migration/fracture** (1-3%), **IPG malfunction**, and stimulation-induced side effects (phosphenes, dysarthria, paraesthesiae, psychiatric effects).

blocks.title: Clinical Context — Microelectrode Recording in Awake DBS
blocks.content: During awake STN DBS surgery, the neurosurgeon and neurophysiologist listen to the characteristic firing patterns through a loudspeaker to identify target nuclei. The STN produces a dense, irregular high-frequency discharge (20-50 Hz) resembling popcorn or cicadas — distinct from the lower firing rates of the zona incerta above and the substantia nigra below. El paciente performs limb movements and counting tasks during recording to identify the motor-related STN sub-territory (dorsolateral STN = motor; ventromedial = limbic/associative — target the motor territory). Real-time UPDRS tremor assessment during macrostimulation confirms therapeutic efficacy at the planned implant depth.

sections.title: DBS for Essential Tremor and Dystonia

blocks.content:
**Essential tremor (ET)** es el más común movement disorder, characterised by bilateral upper extremity postural and kinetic tremor (head and voice in some patients). The **VIM (ventral intermediate nucleus of thalamus)** is the primary DBS target for ET — the VIM is the talámico relay nucleus for cerebeloso input (dentato-tálamo-cortical pathway), and its stimulation interrupts the oscillatory tremor circuit. VIM-DBS achieves **tremor suppression in 65-90% of patients**, with hand tremor reduction of ~60-90% on the contralateral side. Bilateral VIM DBS provides more complete suppression but carries higher risk of dysarthria (speech difficulty from bilateral talámico stimulation). An important limitation: VIM-DBS does NOT improve bradykinesia or rigidity (unlike STN/GPi DBS) and therefore does not benefit patients with parkinsonian tremor in isolation of other PD features unless the tremor is the sole manifestation. **Habituation** (gradual reduction in tremor suppression over time despite continued stimulation) occurs in approximately 50% of patients at 5 years, requiring reprogramming or lead repositioning.

**MRgFUS thalamotomy** is now the preferred minimally invasive alternative for unilateral ET in eligible patients (adequate skull density ratio >0.45, no brain atrophy precluding ultrasound focusing). Tremor suppression equivalent to DBS at 1 year, with the advantage of no implanted hardware. FDA-approved since 2016.

**Primary generalised dystonia (PGD)** is treated with **GPi-DBS**, which has become the standard of care for DYT1-positive dystonia (TORIA gene, previously DYT1, autosomal dominant, early-onset generalised dystonia) and other primary generalised dystonias. The landmark trial is **Vidailhet et al. (NEJM 2005)**: 22 patients with primary generalised dystonia randomised (crossover) to GPi-DBS vs sham stimulation — active stimulation achieved **mean 51% reduction in Burke-Fahn-Marsden Dystonia Rating Scale (BFMDRS) motor score** vs 10% with sham (P=0.009) at 3 months. Long-term follow-up at 5 years (Vidailhet 2007): 60% BFMDRS improvement maintained. Key difference from PD: dystonia responds more slowly to GPi-DBS (weeks to months vs hours to days for PD) — patients must be counselled not to judge outcomes at 1-2 weeks post-programming.

50-80% UPDRS motor improvement, 4h more ON time/day
Koller WC et al. NEJM 1997; Benabid AL 1987 (original)

blocks.title: Dystonia DBS Latency — Counsel for Slow Respuesta
blocks.content: PD responds to DBS within hours to days (dopaminergic circuit modulation). Dystonia responds over WEEKS to MONTHS. This latency is characteristic and clinically important — patients with primary generalised dystonia who do not see immediate benefit after DBS programming must be counselled to wait 3-6 months before assessing efficacy. Early dissatisfaction and premature requests for explantation are preventable with pre-operative education. The Vidailhet trial (NEJM 2005) showed continuing improvement from 3 to 12 months with continued stimulation.

sections.title: DBS for Neuropsychiatric Disorders: OCD, Depression, and Tourette

blocks.content:
**Obsessive-Compulsive Disorder (OCD)**: DBS targeting the **ventral capsule/ventral striatum (VC/VS)** or **anterior limb of the internal capsule (ALIC)** received FDA Humanitarian Device Exemption (HDE) approval in 2009 for treatment-resistant OCD (defined as failure of ≥3 medication trials including ≥1 SSRI + CBT + ≥1 augmentation strategy). The FDA approval is based on the multicenter trial showing >35% reduction in Y-BOCS (Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale) in 47% of patients at 3-year follow-up — an Investigational Device Exemption study of 26 patients with severe OCD (mean Y-BOCS 34/40). DBS of VC/VS modulates the cortico-striato-tálamo-cortical (CSTC) loop, reducing hyperactivity in the anterior cingulate cortex and orbitofrontal cortex. Stimulation side effects: hypomania, anxiety — these are typically managed by parameter adjustments.

**Treatment-Resistant Depression (TRD)**: DBS targeting the **subgenual cingulate cortex (SCC, Brodmann Area 25)** was pioneered by Mayberg et al. (2005, Neuron) showing 60% response rate in 6/6 patients at 6 months — a striking initial finding. Sin embargo, the **BROADEN trial** (large sham-controlled RCT, n=90) failed to meet its primary endpoint at 6 months (response: 20% active vs 17% sham, P=NS), leading to early termination of industry-sponsored trials. Current evidence: open-label case series show 40-60% response rates (>50% Hamilton Depression score reduction) at 2 years with SCC-DBS, but the negative RCT means SCC-DBS remains experimental, not approved. Other targets under investigation: **VCVS** (same target as OCD, antidepressant effect observed), **medial forebrain bundle (MFB)** — rapid-onset antidepressant effect in small series.

**Tourette Síndrome (TS)**: DBS for severe, treatment-refractory Tourette syndrome (motor and phonic tics causing significant impairment, failed ≥3 medication trials, failure of CBIT behavioural therapy) targets **CM-PF talámico complex** (most studied) or **GPi** (internal segment). A small but growing body of evidence (ESSTS registry, multiple centres) shows 50-70% reduction in Yale Global Tic Severity Scale (YGTSS) in selected patients. There is no FDA/CE approved DBS indication for TS — it remains investigational and compassionate use. Patient selection requires multidisciplinary team with psychiatry, neurology, and neurosurgery involvement, with minimum age typically 25 years (given the natural history of tics improving with age in many patients).

blocks.title: DBS for Depression — Failed RCT
blocks.content: The BROADEN trial (Holtzheimer PE et al. Lancet Psychiatry 2017): double-blind RCT of SCC-DBS for TRD (n=90) — primary endpoint not met (20% vs 17% response at 6 months, P=NS). This failure led to termination of industry sponsorship for SCC-DBS research. Despite multiple promising open-label series (Mayberg 2005: 6/6 responded; Kennedy 2011: 41% at 6 years), the RCT does not support SCC-DBS as standard of care. DBS for TRD remains EXPERIMENTAL — it is only offered in research protocols at specialised centres.

DBS complications can be classified as surgical, hardware-related, and stimulation-related. **Surgical complications**: intracranial haemorrhage (symptomatic in 1-3%, most commonly along the electrode trajectory through the putamen); seizures (1-2%, usually self-limiting); infection at lead entry, orificio de trépano, or IPG site (3-5%); pneumocephalus (common, usually asymptomatic); incorrect target placement (requires revision in ~5-10%).

**Hardware complications**: lead migration (1-3% — confirmed by fluoroscopy, may require revision); lead fracture or connector failure (3-5%); IPG battery depletion (rechargeable IPGs: every 15-25 years; non-rechargeable: every 2-5 years depending on stimulation parameters — patient must understand battery monitoring); skin erosion over IPG or leads (1-2%).

**Stimulation-induced side effects** depend on electrode location and spread to adjacent structures: STN stimulation → paraesthesiae (medial spread to sensory thalamus/internal capsule), eye deviation (spread to CN III fascículos), dysarthria, stimulation-induced dyskinesia; GPi stimulation → corticospinal tract effects (capsular spread: sustained muscle contraction), phosphenes (optic tract stimulation); VIM stimulation → paraesthesiae, ataxia, dysarthria (bilateral). **Neuropsychiatric side effects** of STN-DBS: impulsivity (reduced dopaminergic medication can trigger impulse control disorders), hypomania, anxiety, depression. Suicide risk: meta-analysis shows 0.45% suicide completion rate post-DBS (higher than background PD population) — screening for depression and suicidality is mandatory pre-operatively.

**Adaptive (closed-loop) DBS** represents the next generation: sensing of local field potentials (LFPs) at the DBS electrode detects beta-band oscillations (13-30 Hz, pathological in PD), then modulates stimulation delivery in response. **Percept PC** (Medtronic) and **Summit RC+S** (Medtronic research device) enable LFP sensing. Early clinical trials show adaptive DBS reduces cumulative stimulation time by 40-60% while maintaining equivalent motor outcomes, potentially reducing side effects and extending battery life. This is an emerging technology not yet standard clinical practice.
